text: Accumulating 32 minutes of interval work at ~90% HRmax (4×8 min) produces greater VO2peak gains than 16 minutes at ~95% HRmax (4×4 min) or 64 minutes at ~88% HRmax (4×16 min) over 7 weeks.
specific_value: 11.4% VO2peak gain for 4×8 min vs 5.5% for 4×4 min and 5.6% for 4×16 min

text: Interval training intensity and accumulated work duration interact to influence adaptation — neither maximizing intensity nor maximizing duration alone optimizes gains.
4×8 minute intervals performed at ~90% HRmax with 2 sessions per week for 7 weeks improved VO2peak by 11.4% and power at 4 mmol/L blood lactate in trained recreational athletes.
Two interval sessions per week combined with 2-3 low-intensity sessions is sufficient to produce significant aerobic gains in trained recreational athletes over 7 weeks.
4×4 minute intervals at ~95% HRmax produce blood lactate of ~13.2 mmol/L, while 4×8 min at ~90% HRmax produce ~9.6 mmol/L, yet the lower-lactate protocol yields superior adaptations.
Low-intensity-only training (~6 h/week with no dedicated interval sessions) produces only 4.2% VO2peak improvement over 7 weeks compared to 11.4% with added 4×8 min interval work.
Longer interval bouts at lower RPE (4×8 min at 90% HRmax) can be more effective than shorter, harder bouts (4×4 min at 95% HRmax) despite the latter feeling more demanding.
